author: @matildastanford7019
text:
Great video.
l privately hypothesised something like biophoto voltaics would be possible but to now know how much of a reality it is is fantastic.
l also like the location you chose to film it,  l've been reading a lot of Frank Tayell's "surviving the evacuation series.
Kew gardens features as an important location in it.  Good read, even if you don't like zombies.

text: As wonderful as photosysnthesis is, it has very low efficiency converting around 3% of sunlight to internal energy. PV is around 20% sunlight all the way to electricity.

author: @Smo1k
text: Bio-PV is cute and all, but I don't think it's the best use of the sunlight, especially when at the same time we're talking about P2X: Using the sunlit area for cyanobacteria and algae to turn CO2 and water into methane and free oxygen directly is superior simply because you "cut out the middle man". Where bio-voltaics become much more interesting is in light-free, anaerobic processes: Imagine sewage handling optimizing the distribution of earth bacteria for consumption (of the sewage, that is) and reproduction by means of the electricity the bacteria generate in the process, themselves... Less pollution than at present with lower running costs than at present? That doesn't too bad 😉

author: @birdshotbill
text: This is incredibly fascinating, I had pondered using microbes as a power source. It would be awesome to see you make one of these. Maybe the performance could be enhanced by encouraging a symbiotic relationship between plant and microbes. Which might be able to simultaneously generate through biophotovoltaics that then go on to feed and provide nutrients to other microbes/bacteria that also intern generate reducing the need for providing an external input food supply? Thanks for all the amazing and diverse content Robert!

author: @pikotech1
text: In my experience, a lot of chemists tend to shun biology a little bit, so this was a welcome surprise mate. It's been a bit of an interest of mine for a while now. I suspect nature has many of the answers to our current problems if we look hard enough. Personally I have been toying with the idea of bioluminescent algea and photovoltaics for micro generation. Would love to see a working proof of concept on anything bio wise if the mood takes you though. Kew is a wonderful day visit, nice touch!

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
Flora in my native continent of Africa are interesting to me - we have some plants that react with movement to touch or wind (Kruidjie-roer-my-nie) and the Karee trees which limit antelope browsing on their leaves by increasing their tannin - it becomes distasteful and the browsers migrate to other areas - the trees also "communicate" to expel the browsers when necessary.
There should be lots to exploit

author: @veganismyname
text: Cyanobacteria (single-celled algae) are promising as food/energy generators and waste capture of nitrogen and carbon dioxide. One species of interest is Spirulina, as it grows in basic solution with low likelihood of contamination by unwanted toxic species. Many species of microalgae are toxic, such as the ones responsible for ocean dead zones.

author: @dr.froghopper6711
text: Dr. Elain Ingham and her crew at The Soil Food Web school have made very interesting discoveries concerning the microbiology of the soil. You might find their work interesting. For instance, did you know that Brassicas (cabbage, broccoli et al) form only bacterial relations in the soil but no mycorrhizal associations? They do better in anaerobic conditions. That might prove useful for power generation.

author: @orangeswell1469
text: Very good! Will you be trying any experiments in the lab?

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: Not sure it hasn't been done.  Rob previously demonstrated soil batteries -- it would be extended experiment to show that those weren't generating based on plant/bacteria life in the soil: build two that work, then sterilize one of them at >=100C.  If the tested sample stops generating, re-introduce living material from the other sample to see if it begins generating again.

author: @ddbb6618
text: Are you sure the Cathode is positive?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: unfortunately The cathode can be negative like when the cell is electrolytic (where electrical energy provided to the cell is being used for decomposing chemical compounds); or positive as when the cell is galvanic (where chemical reactions are used for generating electrical energy). It's unfortunate because it is not clear cut!

author: @dremaboy777
text:
I'm thinking of making the 'whey graphene' and making a swag generator infused with spirulina powder for the chlorophyll. And testing its results in sunlight, and rain.
Also planning on crushing certain quarts crystals that respond to light frequency, and doping the swag with the fine power. I think the swag (especially made with graphene) is an excellent substrate/platform for exploring electron movement of all kinds. 

Luv ya Rob. 👍🏽

replies.author: @dremaboy777
replies.text:
@yasirrakhurrafat1142 
We had a guy that was also a follower of Rob; that did alot of research and experimenting on energy storage devices (batteries). I think his name is Kev (miss that guy) he stopped making vids. Anyway he did some experiments that seem to show the chlorophyll itself, responded to light. 
Indeed,  that data may be faulty - or wrong; or even I understood what was being shown wrong. I will find out the answer to all this with my experiments. 
What do you think of the crushed quarts crystal powder? Ones that respond to light frequency/waves. 
Because in my mind, 'any' electron movement can't escape getting whizzed away by such a graphene substrate - like the swag

author: @leefoster4133
text: Looked at this some time ago for two years.  Like thermo and static it's long on promise and short of return.  That's around the time I found this channel when I determined it wasn't fruitful and wanted to see what super caps had to offered in storage.  That said thermo does work but it requires a large infrastructure for solar based applications.  Allot of mirrors to heat up some salts to around a million of degrees C or something like that.  This heats water which turns a stream turbine which spins a generator.  It could be used where a magma chamber is near the surface or you tunnel to it.  Never liked that one as it's prone to explosive out comes.  And it's used in some nuclear applications as well which is used on space probes.  Static also works but it's what I call undependable as you just don't know when or how long it will take to build up a usable charge.  You could in theory build a collector on Mount Everest and run those lines to the base.  That would generate power but!  It's going to be regional as you aren't going to get the same outcome everywhere.  But as far as Biophotovoltaics; I see it as a pure pipe dream.  It may not on the surface but you are trying to make a battery of sorts that uses bio-chemical-electrical processes.  That's allot of conversation and if you look at the law of energy conservation; a whole lot of losses.  I was more supportive of the idea to use solar to grow algae to be used in creating bio-diesel.  It's also not as fruitful but far more than Biophotovoltaics.  Good day mate.  Hope everything is going well there and as always i wish you the best.
